countries: United States (South Carolina, Florida, Gulf of Mexico)
nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reef slopes, typically well below 30 m and recorded from about 25 to 80 m depth; juveniles have been observed sheltering in floating Sargassum rafts at the surface.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
securely placed rockwork (this species rearranges decor)

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on small crustaceans (crabs, shrimp) and sea urchins. In captivity, generally accepts a varied meaty diet of mysis, krill, chopped shrimp, squid, clams (which also help wear down the continually-growing teeth), and prepared marine carnivore pellets and frozen formulations. Vitamin-enriched and occasional algae-based offerings are also commonly advised.

avoidWith: sessile invertebrates the trigger may sample

summary: A relatively peaceful Western Atlantic triggerfish that tends to stay under ~25 cm. Best suited to a roomy fish-only or FOWLR system with plenty of swimming space, rockwork caves, and tankmates robust enough to handle its semi-aggressive moods.
careNotes:
The Red Tail Triggerfish (Xanthichthys ringens), also widely sold as the Sargassum Triggerfish, is one of the more aquarist-friendly members of the Balistidae. Adults are generally reported to reach around 20–25 cm, with FishBase citing 25 cm TL as the published maximum. It is native to the Western Atlantic from the Carolinas and Bermuda through the Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean down to Brazil, typically on deeper outer-reef slopes (25–80 m).

A spacious aquarium is generally advised — minimum tank recommendations across sources spanned roughly 265 L (70 gal) up to 1500 L (≈396 gal). Around 500 L (≈130 gal) with a long footprint is a sensible starting point that accounts for this trigger's active swimming and rock-rearranging behavior; larger systems are advisable for long-term housing or community setups. Provide sturdy rockwork with caves and overhangs (securely placed, as this species tends to rearrange decor), a sand substrate, and ample open swimming space.

Water chemistry is forgiving of standard reef parameters: roughly 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.026, dKH 8–12. The species is considered hardy and a generally easy keeper once established.

Temperament is commonly described as semi-aggressive but on the calmer end for triggerfish — it has been kept alongside tangs, large angelfishes, wrasses, and other robust tankmates. It can become territorial toward conspecifics and may sample small ornamental shrimp, crabs, or very small fish, so it is generally considered reef-safe only with caution. Sources note that it 'vocalizes' with audible grunting sounds when stressed or threatened.

Like all triggerfish, the dorsal trigger spine is a structural locking mechanism rather than a venom delivery, but the powerful jaws and continually-growing teeth can deliver a serious bite — offer clams or other hard-shelled prey to help wear teeth down, and handle with care.

Cross-source notes: Tank-size guidance was the main disagreement. LiveAquaria advises 125 gal (473 L); reefapp.net advises 1500 L; community/secondary sources cited as low as 70 gal (265 L). The spread (>5×) exceeds the 2× threshold, so this entry is marked needs_review pending a husbandry-specialist sanity-check on the chosen 500 L starting figure. Lifespan was not reliably documented in any reviewed source. The Reef2Reef wiki secondary URL (originally supplied) returned HTTP 403; FishBase and reefapp.net were used as substitutes for cross-verification.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Tank-size spread resolved. Three independent specialist sources (LiveAquaria, saltwater.aquarist.com, tropical-fish-keeping.com) all independently cite 125 gal (≈473 L) as the recommended minimum. The reefapp.net 1500 L figure is a clear outlier, likely reflecting a large long-term community display recommendation rather than a true species minimum. The previously-flagged community low of 70 gal (265 L) lacks specialist support. The chosen 500 L minimum (the nearest nice-value above 473 L) is well-supported by the multi-source consensus and is confirmed. dataStatus upgraded to 'researched'.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Xanthichthys ringens has not been documented in the available aquarium literature. The species is a benthic spawner in the wild but is not regularly bred in captivity given the tank volumes required.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Catalog/product listing for Red Tail Triggerfish. Provided care level (easy), temperament (aggressive — interpreted as semi-aggressive for this species given other sources), reef compatibility (no/with caution), price, and basic care narrative. Listed adult size 'six to ten inches' and minimum tank ~125 gal.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), maximum size (25 cm TL), depth range (25–80 m), distribution (Western Atlantic, South Carolina through Gulf of Mexico to Lesser Antilles), wild diet (crabs and sea urchins), and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2011). Used as fallback secondary after the supplied Reef2Reef thread returned HTTP 403.

additional.name: reefapp.net Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (25 cm), salinity (SG 1.026), temperature (~26°C), reef-safe status ('with luck'), behavior notes (rearranges decor, makes grunting sounds), and dietary needs (varied carnivore plus clams for teeth wear). Minimum tank listed as 1500 L — notably higher than the LiveAquaria/community range; flagged as the upper end of the source spread.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Originally supplied secondary source from the discovery agent; URL returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on access. Not used for data extraction; logged here to document the fallback to FishBase and reefapp.net.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable records of captive reproduction in the reviewed aquarium literature. Triggerfish in general are benthic spawners with paired guarding behavior in the wild, but tank-volume and pair-formation challenges have prevented routine captive breeding.
